Bonus Q&A: Big Problem and
Bigger Solution Session
ALL CONTENT © COPYRIGHT 2011 AND BEYOND | JON BENSON 3X

Jon: 	So here’s your optional 30-minute Q&A session. We’re going to open
this up with questions. And this is where you can ask any question
that you want, any question dealing with any part of the course thus
far. If you have questions about today’s assignments, it’s even better.
We wanted to focus on those. We’re going to take those first because
this will be online, and we want to have the targeted questions in the
right place.
Let’s start with today’s big problem, bigger solution phases, and let’s
get to your optional question and answer session now.
Josh: 	Alright, this is from Digital Dave. He’s saying, “Can your prime benefit
coin the term, for example, your caloric staggering principle, be a
coined branding term like Digital Dave’s guardian protocol or
something like that?”
Jon: 	Sure, I would without a problem. Without a problem. Guardian
protocol is like freaking awesome by the way. Love that.
I’m thinking about the Digital Dave. On retrospect, my thought behind
that might be it sounds too casual. But you can introduce yourself later
and say, “I’m Digital Dave, and this is Digital Dave’s guardian protocol.”
But if you say, “The secret lies in what’s called the guardian protocol,
and I’ll tell you more about that in a moment,” it’s probably going to be
more powerful. Then when you brand it to yourself, it’s awesome.
Josh: 	Okay. Next question is from David. He’s saying, “Whenever you say
text that is illustrative text, for example pizza, why would you not want
to illustrate that at the same time with an actual picture of pizza?”

-- 1 of 16 --

2
Jon: 	Oh, you would. You could. I made the decision, when we were going
through this, that when I put the template together, I did not want to put
images in the template for a specific reason.
We’ll tell you where to put images here. There’s a short tutorial video
coming out, when we’re through. I don’t want people worrying about
images, right now. I want them to worry about writing. So, worry about
writing this thing.
Where the images go is very simple. That would actually be one place
I would put an image. Being cautious enough to say, if you put a
pepperoni pizza there, some people hate pepperoni, so put a cheese
pizza or something to that effect. But yeah, I would.
The reason I didn’t is because people would be thinking, “I have to
have an image there,” and you don’t. I’ve done entire video sales
letters with zero images, not even of the product.
Now, when we get into the grand offer, we are going to be talking
about product and images and where they go and how they should be
put and all that kind of stuff. But yeah, the answer is yes I would. I
would have one there. But that’s why we didn’t put one in the slides.
Josh: 	Okay, here’s another question but I think you answered this just now.
But it’s specific to clipart. How much clipart is in the final video sales
letter?
Jon: 	Oh, you know what? I don’t like clipart. I mean I personally don’t care
for it. I would opt for photographs over clipart. And how much is in the
final? The major part of the artwork comes in the grand offer, which is
when you’re showing the product.
Where you want to use it in the other places inside your video sales
letter is, again, that is going to be in a tutorial video that will be
uploaded next week, after this is all done. It’s going to say, “Where do
I put my images?” And we’re going to break it into 4 or 5 locations.
And those locations are based on pattern interrupts.
So, having a pizza image wouldn’t be bad there. Like, for example,
we’ll go back to when I said, “It’s pizza time,” I might put a pizza image
there. But that’s not a pattern interrupt. That’s really not, because
you’re expecting a pizza there. Right?
The images that we’re going to use are either pattern interrupts, style
images – obviously used at the first – or we’re going to use images that
just convey an emotion.
So, for example, if I was going to use an emotional-charged image for

-- 2 of 16 --

3
“It’s pizza time,” I would have a woman looking very excited.
So, those are the kind of strategies I want to give you. In other words,
if you have a woman looking very excited, it could be any food time
you want. This is why I don’t want to break it down into giving
templates with images in specific locations, because in that slide I say,
“It’s pizza time, or whatever food you like.” So, what image would you
use there? Would you really use a pepperoni pizza? Come to think of
it, now that I’m thinking through it, no. I would use a woman looking
really excited, using someone jumping up and down. It’s a general
image to convey a feeling.
So, when you think of your images, think of how people will feel, not
specifics, in most places, which is why clipart’s kind of a bad way to go.
Josh: 	Alright, this is from Patrick. “From start to finish, is there an estimate
timeframe for the VSL?”
Jon: 	Yeah, the target range that you want to shoot for is 18 to 35 minutes. I
know that sounds really big. You can go over. I wouldn’t go very far
under. I wouldn’t go very far under. 18 minutes, it sounds like a long
time; it’s not at all. It’s not at all.
Most of the successful video sales letters that I’ve done and that other
people have done are right around the 35-minute mark, 30- to 35-
minute mark.
So, if you’re anywhere in let’s just say 20 to 35 minutes. I use 18,
because that was my first VSL, 18 minutes, and it kind of rocked the
world when it first came out. But since then, we’ve done 35-minute
videos.
I’ve got an hour and a half video sales letter. I don’t recommend that,
because that was more of a webinar-style video sales letter. So, you
want to shoot for that 30-minute mark, right around there, give or take
5.
Josh: 	Okay, so this is from Digital Dave. He asks, “In an earlier session, you
talked about video scarcity. How do you use that when promoting a
book, which will not disappear after the video?”
Jon: 	A book that will not disappear after the video? Okay, we’re using video
scarcity. In other words, you want to say that the video will only be
online for a period of time. It has nothing to do with the product. We’re
going to get into product scarcity in the grand offer.
But the video scarcity is exactly that. It’s your video. The presentation
of the video sales letter itself is going to be online only for a short

-- 3 of 16 --

4
period of time, so watch it now, while you can, or a limited period of
time.
So, I use the phrase “limited period of time,” or “I don’t know how long
it’s going to be online.” All those are valid things. Limited period of
time could be 10,000 years, okay? It’s just you want to convey
scarcity. So, that’s what that means.
Josh: 	This one we may need elaboration on but it’s from Ken. And Ken asks,
“What’s the best way to use a combination of the video sales letter with
a live presentation?” I don’t know if he means video sales letter with a
video of him talking, a talking head, sort of?
Jon: 	Yeah, clarify what you mean there, Ken, if you will, and then we’ll get
back to that question. If it’s someone live speaking in a video sales
letter, most the time I’ve seen that bomb.
Josh: 	Okay, he means kind of what you did at Glazer-Kennedy. So, a VSL
behind a live event that you were doing. Remember you had like
presentation slides and you were doing a live event, live presentation?
Jon: 	Oh, okay, so you mean you’re speaking live somewhere?
Josh: 	That’s what I understand. Yes.
Jon: 	Okay. Well, exactly what I did then. We call that a robotic close. So,
basically, it’s a very short, very, very short video sales letter. So, the
secret formula behind that is pretty easy.
First of all, you’ve already built rapport, so you don’t have to go into the
whole rapport-building mode. You don’t have to tell your nightmare
story, because you probably did onstage. You don’t really have to tell
your dream story. You have to turn everything toward the grand offer.
And then you have to slightly adjust the grand offer.
You start with a simple, “Okay, now you’ve just heard how blank,
blank, blank, and blank.” So you go through what you just heard. “And
I just proved to you that blank, blank, blank,” what you just proved to
them. “And now you have a choice to make.” And then you go right
into the grand offer.
So, all you have to do is remind them of what they’ve seen, and remind
them of what you proved to them.
You should have proved to them that, beyond a shadow of a doubt,
that blank, blank and blank is true. “So what do you do now? Well,
you have 2 choices,” and now we’re right back to where we are right
now.

-- 4 of 16 --

5
That’s really the essence of it. It’s pretty much that simple.
Josh: 	Okay, he says, “Thank you.” Okay, this is from Richard. “Could we
use a video sales letter as the final sales video in conjunction with a
launch, like for that whole launch formula?”
Jon: 	Yeah. Okay, I could tell you my personal experience with that is that it
sucked. It was bad. But you know why it was bad? Because the guy
who I was doing this for was insistent on me making it 6 minutes,
because all of his other videos were 6 minutes. And I was like, “Dude,”
but he was the client, paying a lot of money, and made it 6 minutes.
Completely bombed. Completely bombed.
Did the thing my way on a different offer, did great. But you know
what? People have been set up. Let me tell you the one that did
great, so this doesn’t sound quite so traumatic.
We did one for a relationship-building course, and this is a man and
woman who have a very successful practice. They’re doctors, and
they have this how to keep your marriage, lasting love kind of thing.
Their first 3 videos were them talking, and then kind of bonding with
their audience.
So, what we did on the video sales letter was we started off with an
image of them and a little bit of music. So, in other words, a little bit
different. The music was a pattern break, because the other ones
didn’t have music to it, a pattern interrupt.
And then we introduced them and then we faded it out immediately,
and went into a full-blown video sales letter. But we didn’t need a 30-
minute video sales letter, because they’d already been introduced to
the pain. So, we just reminded them.
Again, this is so much like the robotic close. We went into, “Hi, this is
Ken and Katie. If you’re suffering through this, and if you’ve done this,
we covered in the previous videos how so many people suffer through
this. Remember this. We’ve proven to you that this can be happening.
Well, let me introduce you to the solution. What do you do.” So, they
introduced them to the product.
So, it’s a little bit of a different choice, a little bit of a different pattern.
But that’s how you would do it.
By the way, that really killed their traditional sales page. But the other
way that I’ve done it is to have a full-blown video sales letter at the very
end, and that worked well too.
So, to answer your question, yeah, you can certainly do it.

-- 5 of 16 --

6
Josh: 	Okay, we need a few more questions. So, if you guys can submit
more questions, that would be great.
This ones from Pam. Pam wants to know if you’re going to cover the
difference between a video sales letter and a longer webinar. You sort
of went over that a moment ago.
Jon: 	Yeah, there’s going to be a bonus video. We want you to finish the
VSL first, so this will be in the next 2 weeks. I’m going to put together
a bonus video, explaining just kind of what I just explained to do, like a
robotic close and to do a webinar-style video sales letter.
Webinars and video sales letters are very different. But they use a lot
of the same structure. So, how you’re positioning that structure is
slightly different. I’m going to show you, which no one has seen yet,
I’m going to show you the webinar that we just recorded for this
upcoming product. I’m speaking, so it’s me. And I wrote it, obviously.
It will work as a very good template for you guys. But I want to also
give you the formula that I use to do it. Like I said, it’s different. It’s
different only in a few ways. And one of the ways that it’s different is
that you have a lot more time for bonding and rapport than you do in a
video sales letter, because people have scheduled time for this.
So, webinars should never be under an hour. So, you’ve got to really
stretch this thing out. So, every section gets elongated with the
exception of the pitch. And what gets really elongated is the Q&A at
the end of the pitch. That gets elongated massively.
We’ll go over the formula for that in a video.
Josh: 	Okay, next. This is from Digital Dave. “When do you recommend not
using video sales letter?”
Jon: 	Never.
Josh: 	I can think of one time.
Jon: 	No, I’m saying at the exclusion of all else, never. What I’m saying is
that you should have a text-based sales page, a video sales letter, and
a webinar, and a video, if you like video. You should have all 4
mediums up there. So, you should never not – excuse the double
negative – have a video sales letter.
But, Josh, was intriguing me now. When is the one time you should
not?
Josh: 	Upsell following a video sales letter.

-- 6 of 16 --

7
Jon: 	Oh, okay, I’m talking as a sales page. Yeah. As an upsell, yeah,
yeah, yeah. Upsells work much better – it depends. It depends on
what the upsell was.
I’ve done an upsell for one of my books where I wasn’t the person in
the original video at all, and that worked really well, because it was a
huge pattern interrupt.
But when I was the person in the video talking, and then I went to a
video sales letter upsell, it didn’t work as well as when I just had a
page. It wasn’t that big of a difference, but it was enough to make me
go, “Oh, I don’t want to use it there.”
And the reason is really simple. They just watched a 30-minute video
sales letter. If I’m talking, they may be thinking that I have another 30-
minute video sales letter. So, that’s why it didn’t work as well.
Josh: 	Okay, next is from Justin. “What format would you choose to create a
video sales letter for baby-boomers selling a retirement solution?”
What format would you choose?
Jon: 	I’m not really sure what you mean by format. Can you be more
specific?
Josh: 	While he’s coming up with that, I’ll go to the next one.
Jon: 	Okay, we’ll get back to that. I want to answer that question. So, just
be as specific as you can. We’ll answer it.
Josh: 	Oh, he’s just asking if you think that a VSL would work in that market?
Jon: 	I’m laughing, because it’s like we’ve had this question I think every
session, and I love the challenges. But usually, it comes in the form of
a challenge like, “I’m in the blank industry, so this won’t work for me,”
and I’m like, “Nonsense. I can prove to you. Throw it out to me.”
Josh: 	His note, I think is a very good note, that “Because they’re older,
they’re going to be more skeptical, obviously.” Well, not obviously.
That’s his objection. What would be a little bit different? Would he
focus a little more toward getting over the skepticism? Or is he going
to play out the conspiracy hard, so that he can blame something else?
Would there be anything different about it?
Jon: 	Okay, tell me what your specific market is?
Josh: 	Baby-boomers. I guess I just got rid of your note here, Dustin, if you
could write what it is again. But, I believe it’s baby-boomers and
making money, maybe making money online or __ (15:47).

-- 7 of 16 --

8
Jon: 	Okay, so is the cynicism...
Josh: 	___ (15:51) retired.
Jon: 	Oh, okay. So, where is the cynicism and skepticism? I’m younger
than a baby-boomer, but I’m not that much younger. I’m not really sure
where it is. Is it skepticism?
Josh: 	He says it’s because they’re older, they’re more skeptical.
Jon: 	I think that’s a generalization. I don’t know. Maybe when I’m 55, I can
answer that question, and say, “Man, I’ve turned into a rank skeptic.”
If you know for sure that that’s true, then skepticism is just something
that you would want to address like as a tip. Right from the
introduction of the video. Like when you do the pattern interrupt, you
do the pattern interrupt and I would say, “You’re about to discover,”
and you go through the benefits. And you’re going to say, “And you
know what?” I would say, “Also, I’m going to prove to you, beyond a
shadow of a doubt, that any skepticism you may be having is
unwarranted. You’re about to be able to throw it away.”
I would say something like that, right off the top. The problem is unless
you know for sure that there’s skepticism, that you will implant in their
mind skepticism. So the challenge that you’re going to have there is
walking that fine line of not making someone skeptic who isn’t really
skeptical.
I would be very general with that. So I would say something to the
effect of this: I would say, “Some people,” rather than a lot. “Some
people have approached me and said that they were skeptical about
blank,” whatever the blank is. “So, today’s presentation is going to
enlighten you into knowing why not only is this possible,” I would say,
“It’s all but definite, if you follow these tips.” That’s how I’d breach it.
Josh: 	Another on a side note, the psychology side, people who are very
skeptical typically need a lot of hard-fact truths. So, maybe in your tip
section, you take the time to research to get truths to back it up.
Jon: 	Yep, so you focus on the how-to’s.
Josh: 	Here’s another question from Richard. This is a technical question
about the final, the bonus call. I don’t know why, but there are no more
questions.
Jon: 	Wow, and we have a big audience today. That’s surprising. I would
like to think maybe it’s because we’ve gone the extra mile and made
this as clear as possible.

-- 8 of 16 --

9
Josh: 	Here’s a question from Digital Dave again. “Do you have a glossary of
terms, pattern interrupt, anchor?”
Jon: 	Eww, that’s a good idea.
Josh: 	We have something close to that in toolbox.
Jon: 	Yeah, the VSL toolbox, that’s where I would go for that. That is in the
1-2-3 formula section. And you’ll find the 3X VSL toolbox video that
has a glossary of terms in it. What would probably be helpful, granted,
is to have a Word doc with a glossary of terms in it.
We didn’t think of the mind map. That was actually given to us by Jim.
Jim emailed me and he was like – Jim’s awesome. He said he’s spent
between $500,000 and $1-million on courses. He’s like a really, really
successful real estate dude. He said, “Man, this is, by far and away,
the best course I’ve ever been to,” which really made me feel great,
naturally. I’m just saying. Hint, hint for giving us testimonials.
But the cool thing he said, “You know what would make it even better,
what I’d really love is a mind map.” And I started thinking, “I think in
terms of mind maps anyway. Why didn’t we think of this?” So we just
cranked out all those mind maps this week.
So, we’ll do the same thing here, if other people think that a glossary is
needed. I think that’s a pretty good idea.
Josh: 	Pam wants to know if you can share, even if it’s something that’s a
theoretical in your head, share your criteria or like mental checklist that
you go through when you are trying to figure out whether or not you
should use a video, a text-based video sales letter at this point, or
webinar at this point, or a talking head video? Is there something that
you kind of go in your head that says, if this, then I’d do this?
Jon: 	Okay, you know what? This is where the word testing is the only
answer. Because if you try to second-guess it, you’ll probably guess
wrong. And that goes for experienced marketers as well as newbies.
So, don’t try to guess it, just experiment. Just test it.
So, here’s my rule of thumb, though. If it’s a new product and I know
that I’m doing something right off the bat, it’s going to be a video sales
letter, because they are the most successful sales tool across the
platforms. Not that webinars don’t have higher conversions, because
they do, but they also have lower attendance.
Some webinars out-convert what VSL’s and others don’t. If they follow
this formula, they do really well. But, a webinar is also something that,
again, it’s like if you don’t have the formula to follow, then you’re kind

-- 9 of 16 --

1
0
of shooting in the dark.
So, if you follow this formula, which is really what it’s about, you can
create a webinar. You can even create a text-based sales page with
this formula. You just can’t use some of the push forward techniques
that we use.
I start with video sales letters, and then I add the other stuff to the back
of it. Then, I’ll rotate. I’ll test it. Whichever one wins, wins.
Josh: 	This is an awesome question from Ken. “What do you feel will be the
next generation of VSL’s?”
Jon: 	I don’t even have to guess, I know. It’s the animated VSL’s that are
going around right now. About 6 or 7 months ago, my friend Mike
Geary, I did his video sales letter a couple years ago, right after I did
my first one, and it doubled his conversions. He was already #1 on
ClickBank. So, to double his conversions, he was blown away. He
could not believe it, because he’d gone through iteration after iteration
of his text-based sales page.
And for the last 2 years or 2½ years, Mike has tried to out-convert that
video sales letter. And he has tried everything from like video
___(22:05) style, like imagery stuff, to really fancy Hollywood-style
video, to everything he could think of, and nothing could beat it.
The only thing that beat it – and he splits his audience, male and
female – I think it beat it for the women or for the men, I can’t
remember which one it was now, was this animated-style video. But it
used the same exact script and my voice.
So, again, there’s ways to improve conversions for video sales letters.
I’m not saying that there’s not, by any means. But unless you have the
formula, unless you understand how to write what goes on underneath
it, good luck. Animated video is not going to work for a crappy-written
video sales letter.
But that is the next, that is the huge thing going on right now. And I
think we mentioned this in the very first Q&A. I have no idea what
makes that so appealing. Because for me – I know psychologically
why it’s working. But again, I can’t get past the freneticism. It’s so
frenetic. I hate watching someone draw stuff over and over and over
again, but it does work.
So that is, I think, the next big thing. I also think it’s going to die an
early death, unlike video sales letter and long-text sales pages and all
that. That’s going to be around until the internet dies, hopefully.

1
Josh: 	Which will be soon.
Jon: 	Never.
Josh: 	We’ve got some tips for you to look over. This one’s a little bit difficult.
Let me just skip to another one.
Jon: 	Okay, if you send in a tip please be specific with what it is, what
product it is. Try one, Josh. Just try one. Let’s get a specific one, so
someone will know.
Josh: 	This is a question about tips here. “In the 3 tips section, is it safe to
assume that I shouldn’t do video golf tips, as in like demonstrations?
Being a golf instructor, I’m accustomed to doing video tips.” He’s like
talking live to the camera. “I just want to make sure my assumptions
are correct.”
Jon: 	That’s an excellent question. That would be the only time I would
recommend a video in a sales letter, a video sales letter, actually live
video, unless you know exactly how to blend those together. A couple
guys have done it really, really well.
But that would be one place where you could do a pattern interrupt.
But instead of a video, you might want to consider like a set of shots
that go slide, slide, slide, slide. It goes by quick. So you’re creating a
pattern interrupt, and you’re also giving them the visual information that
they need.
Not like slide, slide, slide, where it’s that fast, but slide, “Here I’m doing
this,” slide, “Now look at my position here,” slide, “Then you want to do
this,” slide, “Okay, this.”
Then you go, “I’ll cover that in more detail later.” Where you cover that
is in your product. So you can go, “I want to show you in depth videos
that go through every little thing,” and that kind of thing.
So, that’s how I personally would do it. There’s something about doing
live-action video inside of a video sales letter that, unless it’s done
right, it creates a bomb. And with golf it may be different. It really may
be different, because that’s such a visual thing.
But that’s another way of doing it is to say, “I’m going to show you how
to do this later on.” It will encourage people to watch the video, for one
thing. So, you may want to try it.
Josh: 	Okay, this one is from Richard. This actually isn’t a question. This
wasn’t a question about tips, this is a tip he had. Richard says, “You
survey your list or the public, they’ll sort of give you information that

2
you need for what they ‘want.’” That’s what he’s saying. It probably
was relevant to what you were saying at the time.
Jon: 	Yeah, that’s very similar to what we had you guys do with the USP
factory. We had you ask your readers what they thought your primary
benefits were. So, it’s the same strategy. I’m taking that’s a tip for
something dealing with internet marketing. But, yeah, ask your users.
Ask your users what they want. That’s an excellent tip. It’s something
that people need to be reminded of, for sure.
Josh: 	Yeah, it’s so simple it’s like, they like to guess. We need more

Jon: 	It’s usually the exact opposite problem we have. We’re usually
bombarded with questions.
Josh: 	I have one here, but I’ve got to wait for a response. If I don’t get a
response before we close out, hopefully they’ll get to it.
Jon: 	Any more questions? Any more questions? This is your time, so feel
free to ask.
Josh: 	Okay, here’s one. What video editor do you use?
Jon: 	We’re not going to cover any of that stuff, sorry. This is all coming up
next week and the week after. I’m kidding, by the way. If I started
getting into all this stuff now, I’m just a little concerned that people
would be start looking at video editing software.
No, I’ll tell you exactly what I use. I use ScreenFlow for the Mac,
because I’m on a Mac. And that is all. You don’t need anything else.
If you’re a Mac user, ScreenFlow. If you’re a PC user, Camtasia.
Those are my 2 recommendations. I much prefer ScreenFlow. But
there’s a new version of Camtasia – Josh and I were just talking about
– that mimics ScreenFlow a lot. They’re both excellent programs.
The great thing about a video sales letter is there’s practically no
editing involved at all. You’re reading text and pressing a button.
Slide, slide, slide, and you just read off.
Now, I read from Word, and sometimes I’ll record – I’m going to go
over both techniques in a tutorial video – literally, I wasn’t kidding
about this part – which is not going to be up until after our last call,
because I don’t want people focused on this.
But in a tutorial video that we have, I show people how I do it 2 ways.
One way is I record. And this is really, really good if you don’t feel
comfortable speaking. If you’re not that good of a speaker and you’re

-- 12 of 16 --

1
3
having to underline certain words, and I’ll show you how to do all that,
and punctuate certain words, then you may want to read this out of a
Word document and record it separately. And then, record the visual
of it on a different track.
That sounds complicated. It’s not at all. It’s really pretty simple. I’ll
show you how I’ve done both. What I prefer to do and what’s, by far
and away, the easiest to do, is just to record right into ScreenFlow. I
have a mic, it’s plugged in, I use that mic and ScreenFlow, and then I
am capturing the screen. In this case, the screen is a PowerPoint
document or a Keynote document. And I’m going, “Hi, I’m Jon, and
this is a cool weight loss tip,” click, go to the next slide. And I’m
recording, right then and there.
And that’s the easiest way to do it, because when you get to a part and
you mess up, you can just pause for 5 or 6 seconds, and then you just
keep going.
And then when you edit the video, you’ll go in and you’ll just take that
part of the video out. I’ll show you how to edit the video in both
Camtasia and in ScreenFlow. It’s very simple. But the editing part of
this is a piece of cake.

Josh was saying, “I’m afraid I know what the answer is.”
The reason why we want you to follow the template is because,

Jon: 	Yeah, let me just tell you the story of Josh real quick. Because no, this

And it’s not that hard to find, by the way, because I’ve done so many of

“And then after you get this done, and after it’s live, then watch other
videos and go, ‘Eww, that part was kind of cool,’ and edit your video
and all that kind of stuff, because you want to try to split-test it against
that.”
But if you don’t, you’re going to be like, “Okay, he used this formula but
he didn’t do that there. He went to here. Now what should I do?
Ugh.” And you’re going to get cognitive dissidence, man. Your brain
will shut down and go, “Ugh.”
So, what we wanted to do to solve that problem is to have one formula
we know works across the board, we know works on – I can’t count
how many video sales letters follow this formula, whether they know it
or not. And the vast majority don’t know. And the ones that have been
successful couldn’t have implemented some of the other stuff, for sure.
So, you’re getting the best of both worlds. You’re getting the best of all
worlds, with this.
Then, when we’re done with the course, yeah, of course we’ll put some
video sales letters in, but just with the earmark there, that you do the
VSL first. You do your own first, follow the formula. I promise you, I
promise you, it’s much, much easier.
Let me give you an analogy, if I can.
When I was obese… Back to weight loss. I know, I keep going back
to weight loss. But since it’s my primary industry, I talk about it a lot.
But I used to be obese. I’m also a voracious reader, and I love
absorbing information. And the worst of that is, like a lot of people on
this call, most entrepreneurs are a little bit ADD. They love to skip
around, “Bright shiny object! Bright shiny object! Ooh, I want to look at
this.” This is our nature. It’s what makes us successful.
One of the reasons we’re successful is that we have the ability to do so
many things, mentally. But it’s also kind of a drawback. In other
words, it’s hard to focus on one thing. So I decided, one thing I had to
struggle with is focusing on one thing.
When I was obese, I would would start reading a book and I’d be
going, “Okay, oh, this is starting to make a lot of sense.” Basically, I

-- 14 of 16 --

1
5
went back through my college nutrition books, because one time I was
a nutrition major. And then I started getting into books I knew people
had been successful. But I wouldn’t read the whole book. I would skip
reading, I’d start reading Clarence’s book, and then I’d go to Jeff’s
book, then I’d go to this book.
I got so freaking confused that, for a year and a half, I did nothing,
nothing. I took no action at all. Because every time I started to go in a
direction, I thought, “Yeah, but Clarence said the exact opposite, so
maybe I better not do that. Oh, now I’m confused. What do I do now?
Let’s go eat pizza.”
I swear, I’m not making this up, man. That’s exactly how it went for
me. I mean I would get really confused and I would just, “Ah, screw it.
I’ll go eat pizza. Let’s talk about it over a burger.” And then it would be
like this, “Okay, tomorrow I’m going to get to this. Tomorrow I’m going
to get clarity on this.”
So finally, what I decided to do is to say, “Okay, I’m going to take one
path, one course. I’m going to take the one that makes the most sense
to me.”
If someone would have come up with a product and said, “Look, here
is the 5-step get lean process,” and walked me through it step by step,
like we’re doing with this one, I would have followed this in a heartbeat.
I would have said, “Oh, yeah, okay, I’ll just put all my faith into this.”
And that’s what I ended up doing. And I got massive success.
And when I started getting the massive success, then I went back and
said, “You know, that one part doesn’t work for me. So I will take that
one part out of this other book and plug it in here.” And I just kept
doing it until finally I found what exactly works for me.”
So, I’m going to teach the same thing. I would be a hypocrite if I didn’t,
if I said, “Yeah, let me give you links to all these different videos, right
now.” I feel like I would be doing you a grave disservice. So I hope
that lecture didn’t come off as a lecture. But I hope that you now know
why we’re doing it this way.

Jon: 	Yes. I’ll give you the best phantom power. We’re getting technical
here. But phantom power, all that means, by the way, it’s a
compressor, it’s a pre-amp for a microphone. They cost about $90 or
something like that. They’re not expensive.
But if you want a microphone that’s a studio-quality microphone like
the one I’m talking into now, you do not need one. Please do not think
that you do. We’re going to give you the lowest end, the medium end,
and the highest end. I’m talking through the highest end. That’s not
what makes the video sales letters convert. I have literally done video
sales letter myself – and I’ll give you the link for it later on, I promise –
where I talked into my laptop. I just spoke right into the laptop. I was
in such a hurry, I didn’t even plug my mic in.
It was a challenge. I said, “Can I make a video sales letter in an hour,
start to finish, have it done in an hour?” And I did. And that video
sales letter, that night, made me thousands of dollars. That was kind
of cool.
I didn’t want to mess with this, so I literally was just typing. It’s not
even that well-written, but it worked. And so again, if you have a
formula you follow, it kind of works.
But back to the B1. The Studio Projects B1 microphone is my favorite.
It’s the best one I’ve ever used. And I use an M audio, mobile pre-
compressor. You can go to ZZSounds.com and find both of these
devices, if you want. The compressor and the microphone together
will run you about $200 something, $200-ish. And that’s like studio
quality, podcast quality stuff. I mean it’s what you’re hearing right now.
And I also have a noise ___ (36:20) in the front of it. If you put that in
there, you’re looking at maybe $250, $300, tops. But that’s if you want
to go the incredibly high route. The medium route cost you like $20,
$30.
